subject: Continuous Random Variables
topic: §38 The Distribution of a Function of a Random Variable
question: Suppose $X$ is a random variable with the following density: $f(x) = \frac{1}{\pi(x^2 + 1)}$, $-\infty < x < \infty$. (a) Find the cdf of $|X|$. (b) Find the pdf of $X^2$.
explanation: (a) $|X|$ takes values in $[0, \infty)$. Thus, $F_{|X|}(x) = 0$ for $x \le 0$. Now, for $x > 0$ we have $F_{|X|}(x) = \Pr(|X| \le x) = \int_{-x}^{x} \frac{1}{\pi(x^2 + 1)}\,dx = \frac{2}{\pi} \tan^{-1} x$. Hence, $F_{|X|}(x) = \begin{cases} 0 & x \le 0 \\ \frac{2}{\pi} \tan^{-1} x & x > 0 \end{cases}$. (b) $X^2$ also takes only nonnegative values, so the density $f_{X^2}(x) = 0$ for $x \le 0$. Furthermore, $F_{X^2}(x) = \Pr(X^2 \le x) = \Pr(|X| \le \sqrt{x}) = \frac{2}{\pi} \tan^{-1} \sqrt{x}$. So by differentiating we get $f_{X^2}(x) = \begin{cases} 0 & x \le 0 \\ \frac{1}{\pi \sqrt{x}(1+x)} & x > 0 \end{cases}$.
